observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Inventory Committee to prepare a Physical Inventory Plan containing the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory taking activities from start up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory.

Thereafter, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin the Inventory Committee, GSO-Designate and Municipal Accountant to undertake the procedural guidelines enumerated in COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances to come up with more reliable account balances to be presented in the financial statements.

Lastly, we recommended the Local Chief Executive to direct the GSO - Designate and Municipal Accountant to prepare PC and PPELC, respectively, for each class of PPE as required in NGAS for LGUs by at least starting with those recently acquired through Acceptance and Inspection Reports, PAR, Disbursement Vouchers, supporting documents and other reports.
observations.managementAction:
pp. 51 – 53 of CY 2023 AAR | GSO manpower were not enough to spearhead the conduct of cleansing. 

The Inventory Committee will be restructured given the changes in functions.



A meeting was conducted which was participated by GSO and Accounting Office on the one-time cleansing activity.

Inventory Committee to re-structure given changes in functions.

To participate in the Nov 2024 seminar on Property and Supply Management System by COA.

PC and PPELC were prepared for currently acquired properties.

Physical Inventory Plan is not yet submitted to the Audit Team.



Preliminary procedures, such as the preparation of the PIP, before the conduct of one-time cleansing of PPE has not yet been started.



PC and PPELC have not yet been maintained by the GSO and Accounting Office, respectively.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct:

The BAC to remind prospective bidders during the pre-bid conference of the completeness of information in the required Class "A" documents, particularly the completeness of declared ongoing and awarded but not yet started government and private contracts and the accuracy of declared NFCC. Otherwise, disqualify non-compliant bidders during bid evaluation or post qualification based on the grounds set under the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184; and

BAC and TWG to judiciously conduct post-qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid; and ensuring that the Post-Qualification Report contains in-depth results on the verification of all requirements by extending the usual post-qualification period.
observations.managementAction:
pp. 57 – 58 of CY 2023 AAR | Prospectively, all bidders are already compliant as to the completeness of information declared on on-going and awarded but not yet started projects on their class “A” documents.




Prospectively, BAC and TWG already applied a more sensible and in-depth way of verifying documents to ascertain truthfulness on all requirements provided by the LCB bidder during post-qualification.
observations.reason:
Implemented.
BAC ensured that in CY2024 bidders  are informed during  pre-bid conferences on the declaration of complete information. 




Not Implemented.
Contract review of infrastructure projects in CY 2024 revealed that one bidder has been awarded with contract despite non-disclosure of all on-going and awarded but not yet started projects casting doubt on the completeness and appropriateness of the post qualification procedures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Treasurer to work closely with the Provincial Government of La Union (PGLU) to streamline the process of aggregating data into the eRPTS by developing protocols for the timely transfer of assessment rolls and other relevant data from PGLU to MAssO and MTO to ensure consistency and accuracy in RPT reporting.

observations.reason: The Acting Municipal Treasurer already communicated the matter to the Provincial Government; however, there was no update on status of the eRPTS.

observations.recommendation:
I.	Derecognition of Donated PPEs - ₱4,151,662.12.

We recommended that the LCE request the Local Sangguniang Bayan to pass a resolution authorizing him to donate the following PPEs to intended beneficiaries.

Also, we recommended that Management prepare deeds of donation for the actual transfer of ownership pursuant to Article 748 of the NCC. Thereafter, cancel the Property Acknowledgement Receipt issued to the accountable officer.

V.	PPE Disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the carrying amount of the PPE that is temporarily idle, fully depreciated, and still in use, as well as retired from active use and held for disposal, to provide users of the Financial Statements with comprehensive and relevant information.

Likewise, instruct the GSO – Designate to identify the quantity and cost of the unserviceable properties and locate the missing PPE.

Adherence to the salient provisions on property management provided for in the MNGAS for LGUs and COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, or the “one-time cleansing of PPE accounts”

We recommended that the LCE direct the:

GSO-Designate to prepare and maintain property cards for each of the PPE accounts based on the AIR and PAR;

Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain property ledger cards;

GSO-Designate and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records, and

Inventory Committee to conduct an annual inventory of PPE.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer observe the instructions provided in the abovementioned regulation with regard to the orderly and systematic issuance of accountable forms.

2.7 We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation and management agreed that
the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Social
Welfare and Development Officer exert diligent efforts to locate the
supporting documents necessary to establish the existence and validity of
the receivable accounts and to exhaust all means to cause their collection,
otherwise a request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts
from the Commission on Audit will be initiated.

3.6 We recommended and management agreed:
a. that the Project-in-Charge and Inspectorate Team refrain from
issuing reports and certifications that are inaccurate;
b. that the municipality hold accountable the concerned Inspectorate
Team members and Project Engineers for failure to comply with
inspection requirements, and institute administrative action, if
warranted.

4.6 We recommended and the municipality agreed to ensure that the
Inspectorate Team and the Project Engineers conduct comprehensive and
accurate inspections, including actual measurements to verify conformity
with project plans and specifications, in line with Paragraph 7 of Annex E of
the RIRR of RA 9184 and DPWH DO No. 166, Series of 2024.

5.4 We recommended and the Municipality agreed to:
a. Compute, impose and collect the corresponding liquidated damages
from the contractors involved in the delayed projects in accordance
with Paragraph 8.1 of Annex E of the RIRR of R.A. 9184.
b. Issue demand letters to the concerned contractors for the immediate
settlement of the liquidated damages.
c. Review and strengthen project monitoring and contract management
protocols, including strict observance of timelines, to ensure the
enforcement of contractual provisions.

6.7 We recommended and the management agreed, through the Bids and
Awards Committee and the Municipal Engineering Office, to strictly ensure
that all future project implementation commences only after the contractor’s
written conformity to the Notice to Proceed has been secured in compliance
to Section 37.4.1 of the Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

7.8. We recommended and management agreed to strictly comply with the
procurement procedures prescribed under GPPB Resolution No. 24-019-
Section 53.14 of the 2016 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184 and COA Circular
2012-001 by ensuring that all fuel purchases are properly justified using
alternative method and are supported with the required documentary
requirements.

8.7. We recommended and management agreed to strictly adhere to the
provisions of COA Circular No. 77-61 by ensuring the use of serially
numbered and properly filled out Driver’s Trip Tickets, preparation of
Monthly Reports of Official Travels, and submission of Monthly Fuel
Consumption Reports to the Audit Team.

9.14 We recommended that the BAC and its TWG, conduct a thorough evaluation
of the documents submitted by bidders before recommending the awarding
of contracts to the HoPE, to ensure that the winning bidder has the requisite
certifications and the capacity to meet technical specifications.

10.8. We recommended that management:
a. Strictly adhere to procurement laws to ensure that all LGU projects
comply with the proper bidding and contracting procedures.
b. Prevent premature project implementation by ensuring that no work
begins before the completion of the required procurement
processes.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct the Municipal Accountant to:
ensure that all actual income receipts without specific purpose be reflected in the Actual Amounts of the SCBAA;
accurately reflect in the original budget column of the current year’s SCBAA the current and continuing appropriation ending balances of previous year’s CO in accordance with IPSAS 24; and
delegate some tasks, such as report reconciliation, to other staff members to improve efficiency.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
Municipal Budget Officer to:
immediately submit the SAAOB for CY 2024 to enable the Audit Team to further validate the appropriateness of recorded obligations and subsequently, the accuracy of balances in the SCBAA;
ensure that the correct expense account relating to the transaction is accurately reflected in the CAFOA; and
delegate the preparation of certain budget reports and the reconciliation of records to other budget staff.  If the current manpower is deemed insufficient, request additional personnel from the Human Resource and Management Office.
Municipal Accountant to:
effect the hereunder proposed compound journal entry to correct the erroneous recording of expenditures, as follows:
restate the CY 2024 financial statements to reflect the effects of the proposed journal entry, as follows:
Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant to:
exercise due diligence in the proper classification and recording of transactions in accordance with the Revised Chart of Accounts, as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009; and
henceforth, conduct monthly or quarterly reconciliation of records to support the accurate preparation of year-end reports and ensure the reliability of information presented in the financial statements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Budget Officer to:
ensure that the under-obligated amount of ₱995,978.90 is not carried forward to the CY 2025 SCBAA under continuing appropriations and revise the SCBAA to reflect the changes; and
thereafter, fully obligate the total amount of all perfected contracts under the Actual Amounts column of the SCBAA to ensure the faithful representation of the Municipality’s financial position, in accordance with applicable accounting standards and budgetary principles, to enhance the accuracy of reported budget utilization and promote better transparency and accountability in financial reporting; and
stay updated on existing guidelines pertaining to the preparation of the SCBAA to ensure accuracy and compliance with applicable financial reporting standards.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
ensure that prior period adjustments are properly presented in the CY 2024 SCNAE to guarantee fair presentation, as follows:
henceforth, if applicable, make a journal entry in the current year when the error is discovered to reflect the adjustments related to prior period errors before restating the account balances.

We recommend that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
prepare the necessary adjusting journal entry to correct the balances of the affected receivables and their corresponding contra-accounts, as proposed below:
review the current duties and responsibilities of the accounting staff to strategically integrate the examination and review of historical transactions, with the goal of identifying potential sources of the recorded receivables; and
initiate and expedite the write-off process by following the required procedures and ensuring the timely submission of all required documentation to the Audit Team for validation, in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to review and validate the long outstanding stale checks presented in the BRS in order to determine their current status, verify whether the related obligations remain valid, and take appropriate action such as reissuance or cancellation of the checks; and
prepare a journal entry to revert to the CIB-LCCA the amount of the stale checks and record/set-up the corresponding payable accounts, substantiated with necessary documents.
Municipal Treasurer to:
formally notify the concerned payees in writing regarding their outstanding stale checks; and
ensure that payees with stale checks are guided through the replacement process once their claims are validated.

We recommended the LCE direct the:
Municipal Accountant to:
ensure that the liquidation of cash advances is recorded in accordance with Section 5.1 of the Revised Cash Examination Manual, to ensure that the records accurately reflect the status of each cash advance and liquidation in order to maintain accurate and reliable financial records;
include cash advances granted to the Municipal Treasurer in the quarterly report on the Monitoring of Cash Advances submitted to the Audit Team;
assess the need to designate additional Disbursing Officer/s subject to the availability of funds for bond premiums, otherwise to strategically plan the timing and frequency of issuance of Cash Advances to the Municipal Treasurer; and
Municipal Treasurer to record liquidations in the cash book based on the actual completion of the liquidation reports and the corresponding supporting documents.
We also recommended that the LCE discontinue the granting of additional cash advances to officials and employees who have outstanding or unliquidated cash advances, in accordance with existing accounting and auditing regulations, to ensure proper accountability and safeguard public funds.

We recommended the LCE to direct the Municipal Accountant to:
strictly monitor outstanding cash advances and require the AOs to liquidate their due and demandable cash advances within the prescribed period, ensuring that complete supporting documents are submitted in compliance with existing rules and regulations;
process cash advances per AO to facilitate timely and accurate liquidation, ensuring clear accountability and simplifying the monitoring and reconciliation of disbursements; and
strictly impose the sanctions for non-compliance as duly provided in COA Circular No. 2012-004 dated November 28, 2012.
Management acknowledged that their current practice of verbal follow-ups for liquidation submissions was proven ineffective. They committed to reviewing and developing internal policies to strengthen enforcement measures. Proposed actions include the issuance of demand letters and, in extreme cases, the possible withholding of salaries or wages to ensure timely submission of liquidation reports.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Engineer to ensure that the CHSP cost is included in the POW and ABC for all succeeding infrastructure projects, under a separate pay item labeled “SPL: Construction Safety and Health” in accordance with DPWH DO No. 56, series of 2005.
Ensure CHSP cost is included in the POW and ABC for all infrastructure projects as a separate pay item labeled “SPL: Construction Safety and Health” in compliance with DPWH DO No. 56, s. 2005. | Municipal Engineer | October 1, 2025 | December 31, 2025 | Not implemented | Engineering Office has inaadvertently overlooked the details of POW and ABC due to voluminous workload. | Municipal Engineer to be instructed to integrate CHSP cost as a standard pay item in all future POW and ABC preparations. A dedicated staff will be assigned to monitor implementation. | 10/10/2025 | Not implemented | 7/1/2025 | 12/31/2025 | Sampling of the DVs submitted for the months of Jan-Jul 2025 revealed that CHSP cost were not yet included in the POW. 

For further validation of the Audit Team upon complete submission of 2025 DVs and supporting documents.

We also recommended that LCE direct the BAC to ensure that the CSHP approved by the DOLE is submitted by the winning bidder prior to the execution of the contract.
Direct the BAC to ensure that the CSHP approved by DOLE is submitted by the winning bidder prior to contract execution.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Engineer to:
develop and implement a system for the regular updating of project signboards, aligned with the schedule outlined in Section 3 of COA Circular No. 2013-004, to ensure that these accurately reflect the actual progress of infrastructure projects, thereby promoting transparency and reinforcing public accountability;
implement standardized field inspection checklists and reporting templates to ensure that project signboards comply with the specifications in the POW; and
develop and adopt a uniform costing template or guidelines for the preparation of project signboard estimates, in order to promote consistency and fairness, and to minimize discretion in cost estimation.

We recommended that the LCE instruct the Municipal Engineer to:
immediately cease the current practice of issuing Suspension and Resumption Orders to contractors without the approval of the HoPe;
require that any request for suspension submitted by contractors be duly supported with relevant and sufficient documentation to substantiate the justification for the suspension; and
conduct a thorough evaluation of all submitted requests for the suspension of infrastructure projects, ensuring that the grounds for suspension align with those outlined in the GPM: Volume III, the findings and recommendations be endorsed to the HoPE for final approval, thereby upholding accountability and proper governance in project implementation.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
GSO-Designate to:
prepare the PO and the AIR in accordance with Section 122 of Volume I of the MNGAS to ensure that all required fields in both documents are accurately and completely filled out;
ensure that the actual date of inspection and date of acceptance are specified to establish clear evidence of when the goods or services were received/rendered, and whether they met the required standards; and
keep abreast of existing guidelines and regulations related to inventory management.
Bids and Awards Committee to:
exercise due diligence in verifying PhilGEPS registration as early as the pre-qualification stage or prior to awarding of contracts;
ensure that, for all instances of alternative methods of procurement, the NOA, Contract/PO, and NTP are duly posted on the PhilGEPS website, the Procuring Entity’s website (if available), and in a conspicuous place within the agency premises, for contracts with an ABC exceeding ₱50,000.00; and
be familiar with the IRR of RA No. 9184, and if budget permits, attend trainings or seminars conducted by the GPPB or PhilGEPS to enhance knowledge and ensure compliance with procurement posting requirements.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain PPELC and Semi-Expendable Property Ledger Card for each type of PPE and semi-expendable properties, respectively.
General Service Officer – Designate to:
replace all issued PARs with ICS for semi-expendable properties acquired from previous years;
thereafter, issue ICS for succeeding issuances of semi-expendable properties; and
prepare and maintain PC and SPC to adequately monitor acquired and issued PPE and semi-expendable properties.
Inventory Committee to:
spearhead the conduct of annual physical count of PPE and semi-expendable properties; and
prepare the RPCPPE and RPCSP following the prescribed format provided by the MNGAS and COA Circular No. 2022-004.
Human Resource Management Officer to facilitate the recruitment process for the GSO position and propose the hiring of additional personnel or reallocating staff from other departments, to ensure the effective operation of the general services unit.
We also recommended that the LCE, in consultation with the department heads, strategically evaluate the composition of the Inventory Committee and, if needed, reconstruct its members, taking into consideration their merit, fitness and current workload.

We recommended that the LCE direct the:
End-User Units to prepare RIS upon request and issuance of inventories and submit the same to the GSO – Designate.
GSO - Designate to:
prepare the SSMI based on the RIS submitted and forward the same to the Municipal Accountant to serve as the basis for the recognition of supplies expense;
request the donor agency to provide the details of each donated inventory item (i.e., quantity, unit cost, and total cost), and furnish a copy to the Municipal Accountant for proper recording in the books of accounts; and
maintain and periodically update SCs using the form prescribed under the MNGAS.
Municipal Accountant to:
recognize the corresponding expenses monthly in the books of accounts based on the submitted SSMI, in accordance with the mandated perpetual inventory method;
apply the moving average method of costing inventories in compliance with Section 115 of the MNGAS;
record all donated inventories received during the year based on the submitted pertinent documents to ensure accurate and complete recognition in the accounting records;
maintain and periodically update stock ledger cards using the form prescribed by the MNGAS; and
ensure that each inventory item has an assigned unit cost and is properly included in the reported inventory balance in the financial statements.
Inventory Committee to:
spearhead the conduct of semi-annual physical count of inventories; and
ensure that all inventories on hand as a result of the physical count, including those issued to end-user units but not yet ultimately distributed to their intended beneficiaries, are included in the RPCI.
Human Resources Management Officer to assess the manpower complement needed in the GSO and Accounting Office and augment employee thereat as necessary.
We also recommended that the LCE require the appropriate key personnel—such as the Municipal Accountant, the GSO-Designate, and members of the Inventory Committee, among others—to attend seminars or training on inventory management to enhance their knowledge and ensure proper implementation of inventory procedures in accordance with applicable regulations and best practices.

We recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Accountant to:
withhold the applicable taxes at the time the expenses are accrued and remit the same to the BIR within the prescribed period in compliance with Revenue Regulation No. 04-2024; and
keep himself abreast of revenue regulations issued by the BIR.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to correct the accounting error to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately conduct reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts and prepare the necessary adjustments to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information in line with Paragraph 27 of IPSAS 1 and Section 111 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Immediately conduct reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts and prepare the necessary adjustments to come up with a reliable and accurate financial information in line with Section 21 of IPSAS and Section 111 of P.D. 1445; and
If appropriate, secure the necessary documents to support the request for write-off of the dormant and unreconciled balance of Due from Other Funds and follow the procedures for the proper disposition of dormant accounts prescribed by COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023 and henceforth ensure that intra-agency transactions are immediately settled and monitored to avoid accumulation of unreconciled balances.

We recommended to Management the following:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to secure the necessary documents, if deemed appropriate, to support the request for write-off of the dormant accounts and follow the procedures for the proper disposition thereof provided by COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; and

2.	Direct the Municipal Accountant to thereafter recognize allowance for impairment on the Loans Receivable-Others balance pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended to Management the following:
1.	Instruct the Municipal Accountant to secure the necessary documents, if deemed appropriate, to support the request for write-off of the dormant accounts and follow the procedures for the proper disposition thereof provided by COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023; and
2.	Direct the Municipal Accountant to thereafter recognize allowance for impairment on the Other Receivables balance pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We reiterated to Management the following recommendations:
The Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary Adjusting Journal Entries (AJE) to correct the misstated accounts in conformity with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.
2. 	Direct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to revisit its existing practices and formulate new strategies to improve its collection efficiency rate on the Municipal Urban Development Project; and
3. 	The Management to initiate a dialogue with the concerned settlers or occupants of the remaining units who are yet to enter into contract with the LGU under the Municipal Urban Development Project and encourage them to avail of the said project in line with Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee immediately conduct physical count and henceforth submit the RPCPPE within the deadline set by the Commission to the Auditor’s Office which is not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended to Management the following:
Direct the Municipal General Services Officer DesignatetopreparetheReportonLocalRoadNetwork, maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map, and keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components;
Direct the Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer with a complete description and cost segregation of road components for all road projects; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare the necessary disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements, prepare the Local Road Network Ledger Card, and ensure the timely recording of completed road projects and recompute the depreciation for Calendar Year 2024 to reflect the accurate asset valuation and reliable financial reporting.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer Designate prepare and regularly update the PPELCs and PCs to ensure the integrity of property custodianship and that adequate internal control is adapted in accordance with pertinent provisions of the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

We recommended to Management the following:
1. 	 Direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize the eleven (11) parcels of land. The properties acquired through exchange transaction (sale) shall be measured at its cost while the property acquired through a non-exchange transaction (donation) shall be measured at its fair value pursuant to Paragraphs 26 and 27 of IPSAS 17;
2. 	Initiate and prioritize the registration and/or transfer of the titles of land in the name of LGU Luna, Isabela to have conclusive evidence of ownership and to fully protect the interest of the Municipality in adherence to Section 39(2) of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 148 of COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October 20, 1992; and
3. 	instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the concerned offices for the reconciliation of the unaccounted beginning balance and prepare the necessary adjustments to come up with a reliable and accurate financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant secure the available documents, send notice to the concerned creditors to inform them to submit the lacking documents to facilitate the payment of the amount due, otherwise, revert to the Government Equity the unliquidated balance of accounts payable amounting to ₱313,606.86 in line with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445.

We recommended that Management immediately remit the unexpended balance of PDAF amounting to ₱3,456.30 to the Bureau of Treasury in compliance with the pronouncements of the Supreme Court in GR Nos. 208566, 208493 and 209251 and in view of Section 29(3), Article VI of the 1987 Philippine Constitution.

We recommended that Management cause the immediate liquidation of the funds transferred and return to the source agencies the unexpended balances and the refund of audit disallowance totaling to ₱342,146.76 to fully settle the obligations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassification of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the concerned offices for the reconciliation of the unaccounted beginning balance of Due to National Government Agencies and prepare the necessary adjustments to come up with a reliable and accurate financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately reconcile the account balances presented in the Statement of Financial Position and Subsidiary Ledgers and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the discrepancy and the misclassification of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant inform the concerned beneficiaries to submit their claim together with the lacking documents to facilitate the payment of the amount due. Moreover, we recommended that Management request authority from the Provincial Government of Isabela to utilize the refunded amount of audit disallowance, otherwise return to the source agencies the total amount of ₱184,628.64 to fully settle the obligations.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the misclassification of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015, to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately reconcile the account balances presented in the Statement of Financial Position and Subsidiary Ledgers and prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to correct the discrepancy to come up with an accurate and reliable financial information.

We recommended to Management the following:
1. 	Require the Municipal Budget Officer to maintain separate Registry of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) and subsidiary records of DRRM Fund in compliance with Section 5.1.4 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012; and
2. 	Require the Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to prepare and submit the monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRM Fund in compliance with Section 5.1.5 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that Management:
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to secure the necessary documents to facilitate the payment and release of the retention monies amounting to ₱173,017.60 pursuant to pertinent provisions of Section 62 of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184. Any amount due to the LGU by the concerned suppliers/contractors shall be deducted therefrom;
Consider reverting to the Government Equity the amount of ₱22,456.07 which remained unclaimed for more than two (2) years, if warranted, in line with Section 98 of P.D. No. 1445; and
Revisit Section 54.5 of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184 and adopt a policy clearly defining the mode of procurement and the nature of project when retention monies shall only be required.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary disclosures required by the relevant IPSASs and the NGAS Manual for LGUs particularly on the Government Equity account to ensure the fair presentation of the account in the CY 2024 financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer avoid over-remittance to the BIR by ensuring accurate tax computations, timely reconciliation of payments and amounts withheld, and proper posting of transactions in the Subsidiary Ledger.

We recommended that Management thru the Municipal Human Resource and Management Officer:
1. 	Submit the copies of the contracts and its supporting documents and henceforth, ensure that copies of contracts are submitted within the prescribed period of five (5) days from issuance thereof in strict adherence to item 3.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009, to avoid the issuance of audit suspension/s on the transactions covered by the unsubmitted documents; and
2. 	The Municipal Mayor or the Municipal HRMO submit the required certification that the activities/services cannot be provided by regular or permanent personnel of the agency to support the corresponding payments of salaries and wages in compliance with Item 4.1.3 of OCA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Person to submit the GAD Plan and Budget, together with the GAD Accomplishment Report and their supporting documents, and strictly observe the period prescribed under Item V of COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2024, to ensure the timely evaluation by the Audit Team of these reports.

We recommended that Management formulate a comprehensive and well-defined policy and guidelines in the granting of donations and financial assistance that would ensure equitable allocation of funds among the constituents and safeguard the government resources against loss or wastage in line with Section 2 of P.D No. 1445.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Treasurer require all its collectors to religiously observe prompt remittance of their collections. Moreover, we recommended that the Liquidating Officer strictly verify the collections and the accountable forms pursuant to Section 30, Chapter 3, Volume I of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System (MNGAS) for LGUs to prevent misappropriation or unauthorized use of collections in the custody of the Collecting Officers.

We recommended that the Liquidating Officer and the Collecting Officers observe the instructions provided in the abovementioned regulation with regard to the orderly and systematic issuance of accountable forms.

We recommended that the Liquidating Officer ensure that all Official Receipts are accurately filled out, including the date of issuance, before turning over the collections to the Municipal Treasurer for deposit to the bank.

We recommended that the management:

a) Require the responsible offices entrusted with inventory items to judiciously
maintain stock cards and properly monitor and update the stock cards to generate
a more reliable inventory report;
b) Ensure that all issuances of inventory items be supported with Requisition and
Issue Slip (RIS) to support the issuance or distribution of inventory items and
regularly submit to the Municipal Accountant for prompt recording;
c) Necessitate the Municipal Accountant to institute necessary adjustments in
relation to the Inventory account and maintain inventory ledger cards for
inventory items and promptly update the ledger cards as part of its internal
control and timely reconciliation; and
d) Review and reassess the accounting policy and procedure of the LGU for
inventory undertakings pursuant to existing laws, rules and regulations taking in
to consideration the soundness of the internal control adopted by the LGU.

In view of the foregoing observations, we recommended the following courses of
action:
To the Municipal Accountant:
a) Prepare adjusting journal entry/ies to recognize all the valid reconciling items,
including the take up the unrecorded bank account and effect the necessary
entries on the restoration of cash of the stale check;
To the Municipal Treasurer:
b) Regularly monitor the status of checks released and unreleased. Send written
notice to the concerned payee regarding the existence of the checks at least a
month before it becomes stale, in compliance with Section 59 of Volume I of the
Manual on NGAS;
c) Return the original DVs and supporting documents of the staled checks to the
Municipal Accountant who will prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record
the transaction as Payable;
To the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
d) Perform a regular reconciliation of the accounts to avoid the accumulation of
unreconciled items in the books; and
e) Analyze the dormant bank account and communicate with the source agency to
determine the nature and terms of the account. If warranted, cause the closure of
the dormant account, and transfer the balance to the general fund to protect the
amount from possible risk of loss of rights and ownership.

In view of the foregoing observation, we recommended that the Management evaluate
the property tax receivable accounts and validate their existence and collectability.
Implement appropriate measures in compliance with IPSAS 41 and COA Circular No.
2023-008, particularly in recognizing an allowance for impairment losses, if
necessary, to ensure accurate financial reporting and prevent the overstatement of
receivables in the financial statements.

With the foregoing audit observations, we recommended that Management instruct
the Municipal Accountant to:

a) Reclassify the current portion of Loans Payable–Domestic for more reliable and
accurate presentation of liabilities of the LGU in the Statement of Financial
Position as at year-end/reporting date; and

b) Provide disclosure of relevant information in the Notes to Financial Statements
for the subsequent years for appropriate understanding.

In view of the foregoing, we recommended that the Management direct:

a) The Municipal General Services Officer (MGSO) to submit the RPCPPE not later
than January 31 of each year;
b) The MGSO to strengthen their controls and improve procedures in the
management of the Municipality’s properties and equipment by maintaining the
required reports and up-to-date records to monitor the existence, condition, and
movement of PPEs at any given time;
c) The Municipal Accountant and the MGSO to ensure that the balances of the PPE
accounts are reconciled on a regular basis and that discrepancies noted shall be
immediately investigated and resolved; and
d) The Municipal Accountant to include disclosures of the Local Road Network
System in the Notes to Financial Statements based on the prescribed format.

1.8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw an adjusting
entry on the affected accounts and record all subsequent fund transfers from
National Government Agencies for the implementation of specific programs or projects to “Due to National Government Agencies” account.

2.8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all fund
transfers to other LGUs with a specific purpose, under the “Due from
Local Government Units” account and require them to liquidate the same after the purpose has been served or before a new fund transfer is released whichever comes first.

3.9. We recommended that the:
3.9.1. Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer:
a. Coordinate with the bank and ensure that the subsidiary
ledgers and cashbooks reflect the actual existing bank
accounts; and
b. Reconcile the internal records such as the subsidiary ledgers
and cashbooks to ensure that all the internal records reflect
the same cash position.
3.9.2. Municipal Accountant:
a. Ensure that the Bank Reconciliation Statement is prepared
for each individual bank account; and
b. Disclose in the Notes to the Financial Statements the book
reconciling items that cannot be recorded yet due to the
unavailability of details and their effect on the Cash in Bank
balance presented in the financial statements.

4.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor establish an Inventory
Committee with adequate members, including at least one representative each from the Accounting and General Services Offices (GSO) to: (a) formulate a Physical Inventory Plan (PIP) per COA Circular No. 2020-006; (b) submit the PIP to the Audit Team 10 days before inventory-taking activities commence; and (c) complete the inventory-taking in accordance with the PIP within the prescribed period.

5.10. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Perform a detailed review and verification of unliquidated cash
advances labelled as “For Recon” and those with negative balances
to ensure the appropriate action or resolution to be taken against
the accountable officers; and
b. Issue a demand letter to accountable officials and employees with
cash advances already due for liquidation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer to immediately evaluate and determine the transaction/s which caused the variance and conduct reconciliation thereon pursuant to COA Circular 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and Section 74 of P.D. No 1445.
observations.reason: Requires continuous monitoring.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the subsidiary ledgers to determine, at any given time, the details and status of the accounts and to facilitate verification of the Financial Statements. Also, submit the required records as provided under Sections 2.0 and 4.1 of COA Circular 2010-001 dated March 2, 2010.

We recommended that the Management immediately submit the required authority from the Sangguniang Bayan for the expenditures totaling ₱5.768 million charged against the "Construction and Rehabilitation of Roads" appropriated under the 20% Development Fund. Moreover, we recommended that Management require the Local Development Council (LDC) to enumerate in the revised Annual Investment Plan (AIP) the specific Programs, Projects and Activities (PPAs) to set a clear disbursement limit and to avoid delay in the implementation thereof.

We recommended that the Management ensure the optimum utilization of the 20% Development Fund by implementing all of the development projects during the year. Moreover, we recommended that Management monitor the implementation of the projects to attain the desired benefits as envisioned in Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2020-1 of DBM-DOF-DILG.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC) to prepare the LDRRMFIP by adopting the prescribed format in the Annex A of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant and the Acting Municipal Treasurer to monitor and ensure the actual cash transfer at the end of the year of the unexpended balance of the QRF and DRRM-MOOE to a Special Trust Fund in compliance with the pertinent provisions of Republic Act No. 10121. Moreover, we recommended that Management ensure that the LDDRM Fund is properly monitored and that the accounting and reporting guidelines on the unspent balance of the LDRRMF as prescribed in Sections 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012 are fully complied with.

Require the concerned contractors to immediately repair, rectify or correct the deficiencies noted in the inspection and conform with the approved program of works otherwise, require them to refund the equivalent amount of the deficiencies to prevent issuance of Notice of Disallowance (ND) by the COA Audit Team; Create an Inspectorate Team that is tasked among others to conduct preliminary inspection once the project reaches an accomplishment of ninety-five (95%) of the total contract amount and submit punch-list of the remaining works, work deficiencies for necessary corrections, and the specific duration/time to fully complete the project considering the approved remaining contract time; and Direct the Municipal Engineer and the Inspectorate Team to henceforth conduct regular and strict monitoring and supervision of all LGU implemented infrastructure projects to ensure that these are accomplished in accordance with the approved program of works and completed within their specified contract time to fully achieve the intended benefits of these projects.

We recommended that Management secure adequate bonding for the Liquidating Officer and Collecting Officer with the Bureau of Treasury to ensure that the LGU is duly and adequately indemnified in the event of embezzlement or loss of funds under the custody of the said accountable officers.

We recommended that the LGU strengthen its internal control on the safekeeping of funds by purchasing and installing a safe/vault where the Liquidating Officer may keep the collections pending its deposit to the bank.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer/collectors/tellers and other accountable officers prepare and submit the individual RAAFs at the end of the month to properly monitor individual accountabilities pursuant to the above-cited provisions.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer ensure that the duplicate combinations of the vault are on file with an authorized officer in the Municipal Treasury Office pursuant to the principle of sound internal control provided in Section 123 of P.D. 1445.

To the Local Chief Executive: a. Establish and direct to the new Inventory Committee to strictly adhere and comply to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 which prescribe the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Disposition for Non-Existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances for Government Agencies. b. Reassess and reconsider the internal controls, policies and procedures adopted by the Municipality in recognizing, monitoring, safeguarding and reporting of its Property, Plant and Equipment. c. Ensure that all insurable physical assets are adequately and reliably protected against any damage to or loss of properties pursuant to Republic Act No. 656. To the Municipal General Services Officer: d. Conduct an inventory taking in the ensuing years, together with other members of the Inventory Committee of the LGU and submit the required Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) every January 31 of each year. e. Maintain proper and complete records of the PPEs, particularly the property cards as mandated under the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I. f. Coordinate with the Accounting Office to fast-track the reconciliation of the inventory reports and accounting records. To the Municipal Accountant: g. Regularly maintain and update the PPE ledger cards for each category of assets as mandated under the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Volume I.
observations.managementAction: AAR Executive: 2020  a. Establish and direct to the new Inventory Committee to strictly adhere and comply to the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 which prescribe the Guidelines and Procedures in the Conduct of Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), Recognition of PPE Items Found at Disposition for Non-Existing/Missing PPE Items, for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE Account Balances for Government Agencies. 2023  The Municipal Accountant and MGSO are in the process of reconciling its records. Hence, this was reiterated and incorporated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation: a. To the Local Chief Executive: Direct the concerned offices to expedite the implementation of the partially and unimplemented 20% Local Development Fund programs and projects to fully attain the desired socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes essential to the promotion of the general welfare. b. To the Local Development Council: Identify the cause of the delays or non-implementation of the PPAs for both current and continuing appropriations and determine whether the projects are still relevant and align with the local government unit's priorities and needs.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2021  2023 The Management explained that most of the unimplemented projects were already reprogrammed, but the new PPAs are yet to be implemented in 2025. Hence, this was reiterated and incorporated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation: Expedite the establishment of the Septage and Sewage Management Ordinance (SSMO) and consequently collaborate with relevant stakeholders in the locality to ensure its effective implementation within its jurisdiction.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2023  Management is committed on the part of making the every effort to establish a Septage and Sewage Management Ordinance (SSMO). This was reiterated and incorporated in Part II of this AAR.

observations.recommendation: a. Require the responsible offices entrusted with inventory items to judiciously maintain stock cards and properly monitor and update the stock cards to generate a more reliable inventory report; b. Ensure that all issuances of inventory items be supported with Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) to support the issuance or distribution of inventory items and regularly submit to the Municipal Accountant for prompt recording; c. Necessitate the Municipal Accountant to institute necessary adjustments in relation to the Inventory account and maintain inventory ledger cards for inventory items and promptly update the ledger cards as part of its internal control and timely reconciliation; and d. Review and reassess the accounting policy and procedure of the LGU for inventory undertakings pursuant to Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and other related laws, rules and regulations taking into consideration the soundness of the internal control adopted by the LGU.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2020 & 2021  2021  The Municipal Accountant and MGSO are currently taking proactive steps in setting up and updating the PPE records. All recommendations were well taken by the management and they committed to ensure that all inventories will be consistently monitored and recorded properly.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive request the Local Sangguniang Bayan to pass a resolution authorizing him to donate the following PPEs to intended beneficiaries.

Also, we recommended that Management prepare deeds of donation for the actual transfer of ownership pursuant to Article 748 of the NCC. Thereafter, cancel the Property Acknowledgement Receipt issued to the accountable officer.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to derecognize the donated PPEs.




Construction-in Progress - ₱795,490.14 

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize the ongoing infrastructure projects and restate the CY 2022 Financial Statements by debiting Construction-in-Progress and crediting Accounts Payable in the amount of ₱795,490.14.  Thereafter, the Municipal Accountant to ensure recognition of all projects reported in the GPPA Report.

Construction of Phases I, II, and III of the Multi-Purpose Building (MPB) at the Municipal Hall

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to restate the CY 2022 Financial Statements to reclassify the Phases I to III of the Construction of the MPB with a total contract cost of ₱8,932,859.31 that were already recognized and classified as Building account and the corresponding depreciation was already recognized.

Unrecognized donated motor vehicles in the books.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the General Services Officer (GSO) - Designate and Municipal Health Officer to facilitate the transfer of ownership of the motor vehicles donated by other government agencies.

We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive request the Local Sanggunian Bayan to pass a resolution to accept the donated PPE.


Thereafter, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to FS information about the donated motor vehicles and record the donated PPE in the books based on the cost or the fair value of the PPE at the time of donation.

PPE Disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statements

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the carrying amount of the PPE that is temporarily idle, fully depreciated, and still in use, as well as retired from active use and held for disposal, to provide users of the Financial Statements with comprehensive and relevant information.

Likewise, instruct the GSO – Designate to identify the quantity and cost of the unserviceable properties and locate the missing PPE.

Adherence to the salient provisions on property management provided for in the MNGAS for LGUs and COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, or the “one-time cleansing of PPE accounts”

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:

GSO-Designate to prepare and maintain property cards for each of the PPE accounts based on the AIR and PAR;


Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain property ledger cards;


GSO-Designate and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records, and


Inventory Committee to conduct an annual inventory of PPE.



We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive: 

Create an Inventory Committee, who are temporarily relieved of all their regular duties, to complete the physical inventory in three months or less;

Direct the Inventory Committee to prepare PIP containing, at the least, the specific assignments/duties of the Committee members, the cut-off date, and a schedule specifying the dates and locations of the inventory-taking activities from the start-up to the targeted completion of the physical inventory;

Submit the approved PIP to the COA Audit Team at least ten calendar days before the scheduled start of inventory-taking activities; and

Ensure full implementation of “one-time cleansing of PPE accounts” according to COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020, to verify the PPE's existence, condition, and accountability.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:

Submit justifications to support awarding of the listed projects despite existence of negative slippage on the on-going projects declared by the winning bidders in their Statement of All On-Going Government and Private Contracts.

Conduct in-depth post qualification of submitted technical requirements of bidders by examining on-going projects if there is a negative slippage as well as conduct ocular inspections on those declared projects to evaluate the ability of the prospective winning bidder to complete project under bid with quality standards and scheduled time frame set.

Since the certificate of final acceptance is yet to be issued, require all contractors to give performance bond for the remaining uncovered period of the completed projects, otherwise, suspend issuance of the certificate until complied with. Thereafter, require performance bond from future awardees covering the required period of validity by Section 39.4 of RA No. 9184.

BAC is already requiring performance bonds on all bided projects as part of contract approval.

BAC Office has already submitted the corresponding BAC Resolution recommending awarding of the contract together with the Notice of Award.

The BAC has required the submission of the income tax return and omnibus sworn statement of suppliers who submitted their price quotations.
