observations.recommendation:
1.7. We recommended that Management:
1.7.1. direct the Inventory Committee to hasten the conduct of
physical inventory and submit to the Audit Team a copy of the
RPCI and RPCPPE; and
1.7.2. require the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to
maintain updated Stock Cards or Property Cards and Ledger
Cards and reconciliation of their records regularly.

2.5. We recommended and Management agreed to, thru the Municipal
Accountant:
2.5.1. effect the following adjusting entry in the Trust Fund Books to
correct the balances of the affected accounts:
Construction in Progress -
Infrastructures Assets 9,713,182.48
Construction in Progress - Buildings
and Other Structures 16,773,367.23
Due to NGAs 21 ,384,833.48
Due to LGUs 5,101,716.23
2.5.2. record/close the CIP account of completed projects to the
appropriate asset account and transfer the same to the General
Fund books.

3.5. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant effect adjustments
to reconcile the understatement of Cash in Bank balance amounting
to ₱490,051.62 as of December 31, 2020. Henceforth, prepare monthly
BRS and submit the same to the Audit Team in accordance to the
provisions of Item 3.2 of COA Circular No. 96-011 dated October 2,
1996.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
direct the Municipal Accountant to adjust the accounts as presented above and thereafter instruct him to account transactions accordingly based on their actual nature using applicable accounting laws, rules, and regulations;
require the Municipal Engineer to provide cost of the demolished parts/structure to the Municipal Accountant so that the latter will be able to derecognize the said parts/structures in the book of accounts; and
instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to coordinate with the Municipal Accountant on the classification of programs, projects, and activities during budget preparation to ensure that financial reports will reflect the actual intent of Management for these programs.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive (LCE) direct:
the Municipal Engineer to furnish copies of GPPA Report to the Municipal Accountant that so that completed projects are assured to be included in the financial statements.
the Municipal Accountant to recognize Flood Control Systems account amounting to ₱564,462.86 to recognize completed project as at December 31, 2020.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to immediately adjust the affected accounts by recognizing Road Networks and the associated Accumulated Depreciation amounting to ₱25,830,825.91 and ₱3,890,722.03, respectively. Moreover, restate the affected accounts in the comparative financial statements of the incoming year as well as provide additional disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements to support the required restatement.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the:
Municipal Budget Officer to maintain Registries of Appropriations, Allotments and Obligations, so he could religiously prepare monthly Status of Appropriation, Allotment, and Obligation and furnish the Municipal Accountant in a timely manner.
Municipal Accountant to reconcile his records with the SAAOB of the Municipal Budget Officer before finalizing the financial statements to ensure that all transactions are completely recognized on the appropriate budget year.
Municipal Treasurer to immediately transmit monthly Disbursement Vouchers and supporting documents to the Municipal Accountant to facilitate timely recording of financial transactions.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:
the Municipal Budget Officer, with the assistance of the Municipal Accountant, to trace the remaining unclassified transactions with the approved annual budget and annual investment plan to determine appropriate recording of the said transactions. Moreover, for him to ensure that requests are included in the annual budget before certifying in the Obligation Request;
the Municipal Accountant to initially reduce Extraordinary and Miscellaneous Expense account and increase the Representation Expense account by ₱240,560.00 in the Financial Statements and adjust the affected accounts accordingly after determining appropriate classification of the other transactions by the Municipal Budget Officer; and
all requesting offices/departments to properly fill-out Obligation Requests to ensure that disbursements for their programs, projects, and activities are duly budgeted.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
Liquidating Officer to consider and record all the accountable forms actually issued for the day and thereafter turn-over it with the all the actual collections to the Municipal Treasurer;
Collectors from the Local Civil Registry, Municipal Market and Municipal Slaughterhouse to remit their collections, intact and daily, to the Municipal Treasurer since these offices were nearby the Municipal Hall; and
Municipal Treasurer to stop immediately the practice of temporarily using the collections to pay petty operating expenses and AICS and establish petty cash fund, and to deposit all collections, intact and daily, on the next banking day to avoid possible risk of loss, misuse, and/or misappropriation of funds.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct all end-users or requesting office to religiously coordinate with the Municipal Budget Officer as to the source of funds of the programs/projects/activities being implemented.
In relation thereto, we have further recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to conscientiously refer to the approved annual investment plan as part of their detective control before further processing the request, issuing the check to the end users, and providing advice for encashment.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to trace the unreconciled inter-fund transactions totaling ₱879,328.99 through prior years’ journals, ledgers, disbursement vouchers through the assistance with the Municipal Treasurer in the determination of the correct balances of the accounts.
Thereafter, require them to immediately transfer all inter-fund receivables, especially those already identified totaling ₱5,477,552.59, to their appropriate fund bank accounts.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Treasurer to judiciously review and authorize transactions based on budgeted amounts to avoid technical issues that would arise from excessive disbursement of funds as a result otherwise.

Moreover, we have recommended that the Local Chief Executive enjoin responsible officers to prepare resolutions for augmentation of savings or appropriation ordinance for supplemental budgets in case additional funds will be needed for programs with lacking budgeted amount as deemed necessary.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee and Technical Working Group to limit claims of honorarium for successfully completed procurements under competitive bidding to the fixed rate per number of procured project as provided in DBM Budget Circular No. 2004-5A dated October 7, 2005.

Moreover, in the event that the sale of bids is not enough to cover the BAC Honoraria, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the BAC to request authority from the Sangguniang Bayan allowing them to utilize savings from project for their Honoraria.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Bids and Awards Committee to:
Submit justifications to support awarding of the listed projects despite existence of negative slippage on the on-going projects declared by the winning bidders in their Statement of All On-Going Government and Private Contracts.
Ensure that all vital information in the Statement of All On-Going Government and Private Contracts are completely provided such as contract duration, date started and percentage of completion, otherwise disqualify bidders who are not complying with the said requirements.
Conduct in-depth post qualification of submitted technical requirements of bidders by examining on-going projects if there is a negative slippage as well as conduct ocular inspections on those declared projects to evaluate the ability of the prospective winning bidder to complete project under bid with quality standards and scheduled time frame set.
Since the certificate of final acceptance is yet to be issued, require all contractors to give performance bond for the remaining uncovered period of the completed projects, otherwise, suspend issuance of the certificate until complied with. Thereafter, require performance bond from future awardees covering the required period of validity by Section 39.4 of RA No. 9184.

Based on the aforementioned, we have recommended the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer to update the Gender and Development Database since data gathering during distribution of relief goods and the DSWD-SAP has already been made.

Thereafter, we have recommended that the Local Chief Executive encourage responsible offices or departments to utilize the said database in developing their programs, projects, and activities to address social issues, such as the negative effects of the COVID-19 pandemic, so that higher probability of implementation and avoidance of delay or cancellation of plans due to mis-information will be guaranteed.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
require from the identified taxpayers additional payment of real property taxes; and
implement tax discount and penalty rates consistent with Provincial Ordinance No. 115-2017 and other issuances of the Province of La Union pertaining to the imposition and collection of real property taxes.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant request for snap shots from the bank every end of the month. These documents could be used in lieu of the bank statements to facilitate timely preparation and submission of Bank Reconciliation Statements to support Management’s assertion on the existence and accuracy of the reported valuation of Cash in Bank – LCCA in the financial statements.

We recommended that Management create an Inventory Committee that will spearhead in the conduct of annual physical count of all properties owned by the Municipality and prepare a report thereon to be submitted to COA not later than January 31 of each year. Moreover, the Treasury Office and the Accounting Office agreed to prepare and maintain Property Cards and PPE Ledgers Cards, respectively and ensure that reconciliation of their records is done before preparation of the annual financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a) Prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers for the financial accounts of the LGU.  These ledgers shall be the source of information to prepare schedules to facilitate verification of the accounts in the financial statements; and
b) Equip herself of the technical knowledge and skills on the preparation of the ledgers by reading prevailing manuals and issuances on the matter and asking guidance from the Auditor.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
Prepare the necessary adjusting entry as shown below:
Prior Period Adjustment		6,299,035.00
Due to NGA 			   6,299,035.00
Ensure that financial transactions are properly recognized and recorded in the books in accordance with Section 112 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2015-009.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere to the policies and procedures prescribed for effective control and accounting of supplies and materials and:
The Municipal Accountant stop the practice of using expense method in recording supplies and materials purchases, maintain an inventory account using perpetual inventory system and prepare supplies ledger card per inventory item;
The Municipal Treasurer maintain supplies and materials stock card per stock number and prepare Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant for updating of its inventory records; and
The Inventory Committee conduct physical count of inventories at least once a year to facilitate establishment of inventory ending balance that will be reflected in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare and issue PAR or ICS to the end-users or recipients for all properties owned by the LGU to establish accountability and ensure judicious use thereof pursuant to COA Circular No. 92-386 and Government Accounting Manual for LGUs.

We recommended that Management strengthen the installation of an effective inventory management system to make sound analysis on the receipt, distribution and/ or utilization of in-kind donations by adhering COA Circular No. 2014-002 as amended by COA Circular No. 2020-009. Require the concerned offices for the receipt and distribution to submit immediately a Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances and the Accounting Office and the Property Unit to record timely the in-kind donations in the books or in the registries.

We recommended that the Municipality refrain from paying additional remuneration to JO workers other than what is entitled to them and support disbursements with complete documentary requirements in adherence to the fundamental principles set forth in Section 4 of PD 1445 and likewise observe pertinent Civil Service rules and regulations. In addition, we require the Municipal Mayor, Municipal Accountant–Designate, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Budget Officer to submit immediately to the Audit Team written justification in relation to these transactions.

We recommended the following measures in order to correct the above noted deficiencies:
The Management ensure that all disbursement vouchers with attached supporting documents are forwarded to the Budget Office for obligation and monitoring prior to payment thereof; and
The Municipal Budget Officer ensure that certification as to existence of available appropriation be done in all financial transactions and prepare and maintain the required Registries of Appropriation, Allotments and Obligations (RAAO) for Capital Outlays (CO), Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE), Personal Services (PS) and Financial Expenses (FE) per responsibility center.

We recommended that Management fill out the vacant position of Municipal Accountant, as prescribed in Section 443 of RA 7160 and in accordance with existing guidelines and rules and regulations of the Civil Service Commission for an effective administration of the Accounting Office which in turn will benefit of the Municipality as a whole.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive and officials concerned ensure that at the start of every budget period, the end-user units of the Municipality shall prepare their respective PPMP in the prescribed format for their different programs, activities and projects (PAPs) and corresponding Annual Procurement Plan (APP) in accordance with Sections 7.1 to 7.4 of the 2016 Revised IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that the BAC submit to the auditor concerned required documents enumerated in COA Circular 2009-001 for the projects implemented in CY 2020 for auditorial review. Further, all future project contracts of the Municipality, together with the documentary requirements, shall be submitted to the auditor concerned for review.

We recommended to Management that expenses inappropriately charged to the 20% DF be transferred and charged against General Fund Proper so that the amounts can be returned for appropriate development projects, otherwise, the amount will be disallowed in audit, being irregular expenditure. Likewise, the Local Chief Executive ensure that the 20% DF of the Municipality be utilized only to fund priority programs or projects included in the approved AIP which partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures in compliance with the provisions of DILG-DBM JMC. No. 2017-1.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive issue an Executive Order or Administrative Order to create a Monitoring and Evaluation Team and the GAD Focal Point System prepare the necessary status and evaluation reports for submission to DILG.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the balance of the Due to NGAs account by debiting Due from Other Funds and crediting Due to NGAs by ₱422,067.08 in the General Fund and recognize Due to Other Funds in the Trust Fund. Thereafter, we have recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer revert the amount of  ₱422,067.08to the General Fund from the Trust Fund.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer account in the Trust fund books all subsequent financial assistance from National Government Agencies with specific purpose in compliance with Section 309(b) of RA 7160 or the Local Government Code of the Philippines for easier monitoring of the fund utilization.
observations.managementAction:
Municipal Accountant made correcting entries in the books for the debit balance of the account and the Municipal Treasurer transferred the said amount from the Trust Fund to the General Fund
Management maintained receipt of financial assistance from National Government Agencies in the appropriate bank account and duly recorded such in the appropriate fund books.
observations.reason:
Review of ledger of the account showed appropriate adjusting entry was already made. Moreover, fund transfer was also made from the trust fund to the general fund amounting ₱422,067.08 on December 2020.
Records showed that financial assistance received from DILG -LGSF was appropriately recorded in the Trust Fund books and maintained in the Trust Fund bank account.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management discontinue the practice of charging prior year’s expenses to the current year’s appropriation in accordance with Sections 306, 322 and 350 of RA 7160. Likewise, for fair presentation of the financial statements and to avoid disallowance in audit, devise control mechanisms in the submission, request for obligation and recording of transactions.
observations.managementAction: Management obligated and recorded MOOE transactions in the appropriate accounting period by implementing cut-off date.
observations.reason: Incurred and unpaid MOOE transactions were duly recorded as of year-end.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to design control mechanisms particularly on reporting (accountability) objective of the Municipality to address the causes of delays in the submission of transaction documents and comply with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and PD 1445 and maximize the benefits of a timely and accurate financial reports and statements.
observations.managementAction: Assigned Job Orders in organizing documents for transmittal from Accounting to Treasury and vise versa. Moreover, Municipal Accountant prepared demand letters to Municipal Treasurer to expedite delays of transfer from Treasury to Accounting Office.
observations.reason: Our records for transmittal of disbursement vouchers and supporting documents showed that the Municipality was able to transmit the said documents as required. Delays noted were minimal to be considered as audit issue.

observations.recommendation:
We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation for the Management to cause the Municipal DRRM Council to judiciously plan its Local DRRM Financial Investment Plan by gathering the necessary information to identify priority projects, programs, and activities and observe proper scheduling to ensure their actual implementation in the succeeding years.
In addition, for him to coordinate with partner agencies specific target location and beneficiaries to avoid duplication of programs.
Moreover, include Disaster Response and Disaster Rehabilitation thematic areas in the allocation of the 70% Mitigation fund and refrain from using the Quick Response without valid declaration of State of Calamity.
Furthermore, review the current year DRRM Plan specially those which do not have a specific target date of implementation/completion to be properly scheduled for the remaining period of the year and ensure their actual implementation.
observations.managementAction:
Management reprogrammed the DRRM Fund to address the adverse effects of the COVID-19 pandemic.
Management implemented all programs and activities funded by DRRMF related to COVID-19 pandemic during the year.
Management included in the Annual Investment Plan all the required thematic areas.
Management implemented programs and activities under DRRM as scheduled
observations.reason:
Programs and activities identified for the said fund were all deemed highly necessary to address the adverse effects of COVID-19 pandemic to families in the Municipality.
All programs and activities identified for the above-mentioned purpose were implemented as planned.
Post audit of transactions showed that programs and activities were implemented as scheduled.
Post audit of transactions showed that programs and activities were implemented as scheduled.

observations.recommendation:
Based from the foregoing, we have recommended Management the following:

The expenditures in relation to the implementation of ALS should not be charged under the Special Education Fund but rather under the funds of the Department of Education. Further, we recommended that all expenditures should be spent solely for public purposes and as to those expenditures under SEF, the utilization should be confined only to those expenditures in consonance with the guidelines of Sections 272 and 100((c) of RA 7160 and Section 7(b) of RA 10410.
Discontinue the practice of charging prior period expenditures against current year appropriations and all procurement shall conform with the provisions of RA 9184.
Moreover, prior to awarding of contracts, secure completeness of the necessary eligibility documents from the supplier with the lowest quotation to ensure continuous processing of documents until payment.
observations.managementAction:
Management did not charge in the Special Education Fund payment of wages for ALS implementors.
Payment to transactions from previous year was duly obligated in the previous year.
Management secured completeness of supporting documents for SEF- related transactions.
observations.reason:
Post audit of transactions showed that no payment was made for ALS-related transactions.
Post audit of transactions showed that there was no expenditure charged in the current appropriation from previous year’s transactions.
Post audit of transactions also showed that supporting documents for CY 2020 SEF transactions were complete.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Treasury and Municipal Accounting Offices be required to:

[a] trace the nature of the accounts Due from NGAs and Due from LGUs and send demand letters, if necessary or effect adjustment to its appropriate account classification;

[b] for Other Receivable and Loans Receivable accounts, exert effort to locate the current addresses of the borrowers and send demand letters; and

[c] if the probability of collection of these accounts is remote, file request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts to COA Office, duly supported with documents mentioned under paragraph 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
observations.managementAction:
AOM No. 2019-05 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 7 | The management commented that such accounts cannot be specifically identified since no ledger was turned-over by the former Municipal Accountant.  As per inquiry with the former Municipal Accountant, such balances were the results of the transition from the old Government Accounting System (OGAS) to the New Government Accounting System (NGAS) but no records/documents was provided to support her claim.
The recommendation of request for write-off is also remote due to the inability of the management to provide the necessary Documents required for its write-off.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accounting office review the existing loans payable account and determine the veracity of the booked-up loan from the Department of Agriculture then make representation with the latter for the return of the equipment as embodied in the loan agreement.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-10 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 12 | Management commented that the recognized liability from the Department of Agriculture is supposed to be dole-out but then turned out to be a loan.  The management then, without intention to pay such, requested to impose the clause on the MOA which states that: “In the case of non-payment of two (2) amortization schedules, the DA-RFU 02 has the right to retrieve the machinery/equipment from the recipient.”  Because of this, management wanted that the DA should retrieve the machinery and derecognize the loans payable account.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to include these above-mentioned properties in the LGU books of accounts by drawing the necessary adjusting journal entries taking up these PPE account to its appropriate account classification.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the
The General Services Officer to prepare and maintain the required Property Cards and renew the AREs in order to update his records;
The Inventory Committee to conduct inventory and use the updated records of the GSO and Municipal Accountant to facilitate conduct of inventory and preparation of report; and
The Municipal Accountant and GSO-Designate to regularly reconcile PPE records with the RPCPPE as required in Section 57 of MNGAS to ensure that records maintained are complete and accurate and assets recorded are actually existing.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to recognize in the books the unrecorded road networks with their proportionate Accumulated Depreciation. Moreover, review reports and disbursement vouchers from previous years for similar transactions incurred in Due to NGAs account and appropriately account them in the book of accounts to ensure that all local road transactions are fairly presented in the Financial Statements.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
Municipal Accountant to trace and duly account local road network transactions prior to CY 2016 through journals, disbursement vouchers, supporting documents and other reports. Thereafter, provide the necessary data to the Municipal Engineer and GSO in the conduct of physical inventory of local roads;
Further, for him to include in the Financial Statements the necessary disclosures related to Local Road Networks (Annex B) as well as prepare subsidiary ledgers for the different road components to be reconciled with the property cards of the GSO-Designate as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015.
GSO-Designate with the Inventory Committee to finalize the inventory of local roads including the inventory of road components using the prescribed formats, Annexes A and C of COA Circular No. 2015-008.
Municipal Engineer to provide the Municipal Accountant and GSO- Designate the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct:
The Municipal Treasurer to record those presented in Annex B to have a more reliable cashbook.  Likewise, ensure that all transactions pertaining to cash are recorded timely in the cashbook and all checks issued are recorded in the Report of Checks Issued and submitted to the Municipal Accountant;
The Municpal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer in timely recording the transactions of the Municipality.
Further, we recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer to reconcile their records and bank statements regularly and make the necessary adjustments based on reconciliation made.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the discrepancies noted and remit to the concerned agencies the balances of amount withheld to avoid penalties and surcharges.  Likewise, we recommended that Management remit their contributions within the prescribed period.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause the Municipal Budget Officer to immediately obligate programs/projects in full amount of contract cost upon completion of the said projects. Thereafter, for the Municipal Accountant to duly recognize Guarantee/Security Deposits Payable to reflect the actual and true nature of the transactions pertaining to infrastructure projects.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Accountant to correct the balance of the Due to NGAs account by debiting Due from Other Funds and crediting Due to NGAs by ₱422,067.08 in the General Fund and recognize Due to Other Funds in the Trust Fund.
Thereafter, we have recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer revert the amount of  ₱422,067.08to the General Fund from the Trust Fund.
Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer account in the Trust fund books all subsequent financial assistance from National Government Agencies with specific purpose in compliance with Section 309(b) of RA 7160 or the Local Government Code of the Philippines for easier monitoring of the fund utilization.

We have recommended that management discontinue the practice of charging prior year’s expenses to the current year’s appropriation in accordance with Sections 306, 322 and 350 of RA 7160. Likewise, for fair presentation of the financial statements and to avoid disallowance in audit, devise control mechanisms in the submission, request for obligation and recording of transactions.

We have recommended that the Municipal Mayor consider filling up the vacant position in the Treasury Office and to direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Recognize and reclassify the RPT share for Special Education Fund upon collection and facilitate immediate deposit thereof;
Stop immediately the practice of temporarily using the collection to pay petty operating expenses and AICS and establish petty cash fund; and
Deposit all collections intact daily or the next banking day to avoid possible risk of loss and/or misappropriation of funds.

Based on the foregoing observations, we have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to monitor all cash advances, including those of the disbursing officer; make the necessary adjustments to reflect the true balance of the Advances accounts; and thereafter, record only the amount liquidated and immediately verify the liquidation documents upon submission and prior to granting of another cash advance.
Moreover, direct the Municipal Treasurer to locate and submit immediately the liquidation documents of the unliquidated cash advances; refrain from transferring his cash advances to another; and thereafter, liquidate within the prescribed period and submit immediately the liquidation documents to the Municipal Accountant.
Likewise, we have recommended that Management:
Avoid paying thru cash;
Adopt public bidding as a general mode of procurement;
Refrain from granting cash advance to a single employee or official in behalf of a group of employees/officials; and
Require those who are granted cash advances to immediately liquidate within the prescribed period to avoid withholding of their salary and/or other sanctions.

We have recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity direct the Bids and Awards Committee and Technical Working Group to conduct post qualification by verifying, validating, and ascertaining all statements made and documents submitted by the bidder with the Lowest Calculated Bid and ensure that the Post-Qualification Report contains in-depth results on the verification of technical requirements.
Instruct the Bids and Awards Committee and Technical Working Group, in cases there would be simultaneous or overlapping projects to be awarded to the same contractor, to require the contractor to augment its key personnel and equipment to be committed to the project in order to properly determine the responsiveness of the LCB to the eligibility and bid requirements.  Otherwise, disqualify future bids whose key personnel and equipment are already pledged / used in other on-going projects.
Also, direct the Municipal Engineer to monitor simultaneous implementation of projects by the same contractor to avoid unnecessary delays or substandard quality of work.

In view of the foregoing, we have recommended the management to:
Require the Implementing/End-User Units to prepare the PPMPs in accordance with Section 7 of RA 9184 using the suggested format by the GPPB duly posted in its website and include all infrastructure projects in the preparation of PPMP;
Require the Budget Office to thoroughly review and evaluate the submitted PPMPs in terms of amount and its coherence with the proposed budget of the LGU; and
Require the BAC through the BAC Secretariat to consolidate the PPMPs and prepare the APP in accordance to the applicable provisions of RA 9184 using the prescribed format under GPPB Circular No. 07-2015 and GPPB Resolution No. 20-2015 dated July 16, 2015.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant to design control mechanisms particularly on reporting (accountability) objective of the Municipality to address the causes of delays in the submission of transaction documents and comply with the pertinent provisions of COA Circular Nos. 2009-001 and PD 1445 and maximize the benefits of a timely and accurate financial reports and statements.

We have reiterated our previous year’s recommendation for the Management to cause the Municipal DRRM Council to judiciously plan its Local DRRM Financial Investment Plan by gathering the necessary information to identify priority projects, programs, and activities and observe proper scheduling to ensure their actual implementation in the succeeding years.

In addition, for him to coordinate with partner agencies specific target location and beneficiaries to avoid duplication of programs.

Moreover, include Disaster Response and Disaster Rehabilitation thematic areas in the allocation of the 70% Mitigation fund and refrain from using the Quick Response without valid declaration of State of Calamity.

Furthermore, review the current year DRRM Plan specially those which do not have a specific target date of implementation/completion to be properly scheduled for the remaining period of the year and ensure their actual implementation.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
Automatically release the Special Education Tax Income to the SEF and timely recognize in the cashbooks the collections and deposits; and
Prepare and submit Abstract of Collection for Real Property Tax to the Municipal Accountant regularly to facilitate review of distribution of real property tax collection.

Based from the foregoing, we have recommended Management the following:
The expenditures in relation to the implementation of ALS should not be charged under the Special Education Fund but rather under the funds of the Department of Education. Further, we recommended that all expenditures should be spent solely for public purposes and as to those expenditures under SEF, the utilization should be confined only to those expenditures in consonance with the guidelines of Sections 272 and 100((c) of RA 7160 and Section 7(b) of RA 10410.
Discontinue the practice of charging prior period expenditures against current year appropriations and all procurement shall conform with the provisions of RA 9184.
Moreover, prior to awarding of contracts, secure completeness of the necessary eligibility documents from the supplier with the lowest quotation to ensure continuous processing of documents until payment.

We have recommended that management expedite its action on the findings and recommendations of the Office of the Sangguniang Panlalawigan in order to adjust/update the municipality’s taxes and rates pursuant to Section 191 of Republic Act 7160 and eventually, increase its local income to finance various programs/projects/activities that are beneficial to its constituents. Thereafter, monitor the passage of ordinances to ensure that these are approved and implemented without delay.

We have recommended that Management require:
Additional payment for the uncollected penalties during the CY 2019. Thereafter, strictly implement with the penalty provisions of the current Tax Ordinance, requiring assessment and collection of penalties for delayed payments of business taxes as additional income or funds for projects to be implemented as well as ensure earlier collection of the said funds; and
The certified copy of Annual Income Tax Return to Business Taxpayers on the second quarter of the year and adjust the business tax and collect the difference if there are discrepancies between the amount appearing in the ITR and gross sales declared in the business tax application form.

We have recommended that Management strategically schedule GAD projects and vigilantly monitor its implementation so that its purpose will be actually realized and enjoyed by majority of the community during the year.
Moreover, we recommended that Management update their GAD Database with latest necessary data required in Annex B of JMC 2013-001 to have a more reliable basis in identifying priority gender issues to be addressed for the coming year. Thereafter, judiciously conduct gender analysis using the updated GAD Database in identifying priority gender issues and formulating high-impact programs/projects/activities to address such during planning and budgeting of GAD Funds.

We recommended that management:
ensure that all officials/employees settle their accounts within the time set by pertinent rules and regulations; and
if the probability of collection for the amount of ₱368,061.40 is remote, file request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts to COA Office, duly supported with documents mentioned under paragraph 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.  Otherwise, absent of said documents enumerated in this Circular, management may file appropriate charge against this former accountable officer, if warranted.

We recommended management to require the Municipal Accountant to transfer the unexpended/unobligated QRF and MF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund under the Trust Liability-DRRM account as required under COA Circular No. 2012-02.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations that the Municipal Accountant be required to:
Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and
Perform constant review and appraisal of transactions to deter error and omissions.

We reiterated our recommendation that management should make representation with the DA-RFU-02 for the return of the equipment as embodied in the loan agreement.   Coordinate with the said agency for the issuance of clearance as basis in dropping loans payable account in the books.

With these afore-cited observations, we recommended that management comply with the provision of Budget Circular No. 2007-3 and Budget Circular No. 2004-5A on the payment of honoraria.  We further recommended that the BAC members, TWG and BAC Secretariat be required to refund the excess amount received.   Moreover, the applicable tax be applied for the said honoraria.

We recommended management to:
Submit Monthly Report of Official Travels for every motor vehicle, duly prepared by the Driver concerned summarizing in chronological order his trips for the month; and
Submit Monthly Report of Fuel Consumption for every government motor transportation containing among others, the different types of motor vehicles utilized by the LGU during the month, total distance traveled, total fuel used and the normal fuel consumption for each vehicle.
We recommended management to ensure that the trip tickets are properly accomplished and duly approved by authorized officials before embarking on a trip.

We recommended that management immediately transmit the original copies of documents specified in Annexes A and C of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and other documents, which were not submitted, to facilitate the conduct of technical review and inspection of these eleven (11) infrastructure contracts and to determine the reasonableness of the costs.

We recommended that management require the personnel in-charge in documenting transactions to immediately submit the lacking documents enumerated in the table as basis in the conduct of evaluation.
We recommended management to prepare work program and statement of work accomplished in order to monitor its physical against financial accomplishments and to substantiate the expenditures for fuel used by various equipment in the implementation of various projects and wages of laborers assigned in river control management.
We recommended that management strictly adhere with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 in the preparation of Annual Investment Plan to ensure that all items to be funded are development programs/projects and activities that partakes the nature of investment or capital expenditures.

We recommended that management submit/furnish copies of contracts and Purchase Orders within five (5) days upon approval and Notices of Delivery and Inspection and Acceptance Reports within twenty-four (24) hours from receipt of delivery to the Audit Team for review. Strictly observe the provision of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular No. 96-010 on the submission of all future contracts and its supporting documents including copies of notices of deliveries, inspection and acceptance within the stated period.

We commended management for satisfactorily accomplishing the Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) of the Municipality and recommended to undertake best practices in adhering and implementing the provisions of the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations.
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the person liable for the disallowed amount of ₽5,000.00 to immediately settle the account including settlement of the old account.

We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Accountant, identify the road network where the completed project belongs and make the necessary adjustments in the book of accounts of the Municipality.

Municipal Accountant

Prepare the Journal Voucher (JV) to record the beginning balance of the local road network and its components in the general ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card (Annex D of the Circular), respectively;

Municipal General Services Officer

Maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map; and

Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. (Annex E of the Circular)

Municipal Engineer

Provide the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.

observations.recommendation: 1.a. In addition, the Municipal Budget Officer to update his records more often for all obligated transactions. Further, for them to reconcile their respective records at least every quarter to ensure that all transactions made will be duly accounted for. Any error, omission or misstatement appearing in the books of accounts shall be the joint responsibility of the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant.

observations.recommendation:

Establish Petty Cash Fund through a Sanggunniang Bayan Resolution withdrawn from the general fund for emergency and petty operating expenses consisting of small payments for maintenance and operating expenses which cannot be paid conveniently by check.  Replenish the same with the actual amount of expenditures when it already reached at least 75% of the amount established by submitting a replenishment voucher with all supporting documents duly summarized in a report of disbursements;
 
b.	 Make direct payment through check to the suppliers for transactions exceeding P15,000.00; and

c.	Refrain from paying regular expenses like financial assistance from the petty cash fund.

c.	If after all the possible means were exerted and the claims could not be accounted, request for write-off of the above receivables supported by the above-cited documents and schedules.

observations.reason: Scrutiny of accomplishment report showed that 18 out of 23 GAD projects were not fully implemented during the year. Details are discussed in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the officials concerned closely monitor the cash inflows against outflows using Cash Flow Analysis to ensure that no cash overdraft is incurred and that trust funds are used only for its intended purposes.  Prepare cash analysis including proof of cash schedule in order to reconcile cash against actual payables and to improve the cash position of the municipality.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-01 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 1 | Management justified that the difference between the cash balance and the current obligations of the municipality pertains to the liability account in Trust Fund that were already disbursed however, these remained in the books pending submission of liquidation reports from the source agency considering that the project is still on-going as at December 31, 2018.  These were accounted under “Construction-in-Progress account instead of debiting the liability account    to reduce the balance of payables. Nevertheless, management already made the liquidation hence, the corresponding adjustment on the Liability Account has been made.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should communicate with the concerned payees for their information as to the existence of unissued checks and draw an Adjusting Journal Entry recognizing payable account for the equivalent cost of these checks.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-02 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 2 | The management already communicated to the concerned Payee for their information but replied that said Check Payment were already deposited intact and no problem was encountered on it. However, as per inquiry with the Drawee Bank (LBP), the checks deposit were returned to the Payees.

AOM No. 2019-03 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 3 | The Management already made reconciliation with its subsidiary record against the Cashbook however the reconciling items were not yet adjusted/reflected in the Cashbook. The subsidiary records have already been reconciled against the Bank Balance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the designated Special Disbursing Officer be required to strictly follow the guidelines in the utilization of cash advances and stop the practice of granting cash vales as well as accommodating checks pursuant to the afore-cited provision of COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: AAR 2018 Finding No. 5 | Management complied with the audit recommendation.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure proper grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances by instructing the Municipal Accountant to:

[a] strictly monitor the cash advances of officials/employees before granting another one;

[b] make sure that said officials/employees settle their accounts within the time set by pertinent rules and regulations; and

[c] exert all efforts to enforce settlement of overdue accounts.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-04 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 6 | The management strictly monitor the granting of Cash Advance and ensured that all the existing Cash Advances granted during the current administration has been liquidated. They also exerted efforts to enforce settlement of the overdue account particularly for the account of Mr. Carlos Lampitoc, former employee of LGU Luna, but to no avail. Said accountable officer claimed that such were properly disbursed but have no documents to present to prove his claim.

AOM No. 2019-06 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 8 | The management commits to undertake Physical Inventory Count of Property and Equipment to reconcile the said records. As to the Unserviceable properties of the LGU, the management has a pending request for its Disposal.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:

[a] Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of all Public Infrastructure assets under the LGU’s jurisdiction.  For Road Network account, use the format prescribed under COA Circular No. No. 2015-008.  Ensure that all the fields in the form be properly filled up and furnish the Audit Team a copy as basis in validating the correctness of such accounts;

[b] Direct the Municipal Accountant to fully identify these projects, determine proper classification of these accounts based on the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Sector prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009, then make the necessary adjusting entry to transfer the CIP-Infrastructure Assets account and provide for depreciation.

[c] Determine all Public Infrastructure assets transferred to RPI which are not yet fully depreciated or impaired to be restored and recorded in the books as part of PPE accounts; and

[d] Ensure compliance with accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 by directing the key officials to perform the duties and responsibilities assigned to them.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-07 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 9 | Management justified that there were no records maintained by the former Municipal Accountant and they have no basis to trace the nature of these accounts. The conduct of Inventory of all Public Infrastructure was undertaken by the Office of the Municipal Engineer.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review/verify the Due to NGAs and Other Payable accounts and to draw the necessary adjustments, if any, and to maintain subsidiary ledger detailing all the information under these accounts.  If no record can be found, request authority from authorized officials for the reversion of these accounts to General Fund.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-08 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 10 | Management cited that these accounts (Other Payables and Trust Liabilities under General Fund) has been dormant for many years since no record was turned over by the previous Municipal Accountant and no claims from creditors were received since then, or should there be claimants, demand letter should already be received by management.  Hence, management agreed to request authority from authorized office for the reversion of Other Payables and Trust Liabilities to General Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be required to:

[a] Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and

[b] Perform constant review and appraisal of transactions to deter error and omissions.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-09 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 11 | Management commits to prepare a reconciliation of the accounts Due to and Due from Other Funds and make the necessary adjustments to ensure proper recording and to perform constant review to every transaction involving the said account to avoid incurrence of similar errors.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management revisit the work and financial plan and determine reason/s why until to date, the building remained unutilized.  We further recommended that power connections be checked and determine why it incurred an average monthly electrical bill of more than ₱2,000.00 despite its non-utilization.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-11 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 13 | As ensured by the management during the Exit Conference, the OTOP building is now Operational starting July 2019.
AAR 2018 Finding No. 15 | Distribution process was properly checked.

observations.recommendation: We recommended management to cause the immediate return of unutilized balance to the Source Agency or to the Bureau of Treasury (BTr) as required in paragraph 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-13 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 16 | Remitted to the Source Agency or to the Bureau of Treasury.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management:

[a] require the concerned barangays who were recipients of the fund transfer/financial assistance totaling ₱1,800,000.00 to submit fund utilization report duly certified by the concerned barangay officials together with the inspection report and certificate of project completion; and

[b] adjust the Donations account or the Government Equity account and take up the Due from LGUs account to recognize the accountability and responsibility of the concerned barangay over the project and furnish the Audit Team a copy of the journal entry voucher.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-14 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 17 | The management already required the Barangay to submit fund utilization report for the transfer/financial assistance they received from the Municipality duly certified by the barangay officials concerned.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed to:

[a] strictly adhere with DILG and DBM JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 in the preparation of Annual Investment Plan to be funded out of the 20% Development Fund to ensure that all items to be funded are development programs/projects and activities; and

[b] prepare work program prior to undertaking the projects and statement of work accomplish in order to monitor its physical against financial accomplishments.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-15 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 18 | The management already took into consideration the said reminder to adhere strictly on the guidelines on the preparation of AIP to be funded out of 20% EDF. On the other hand, the program of works and Statement of Work Accomplished required for the expenses incurred for the various undertakings out of 20% EDF were already submitted by the Engineering Office.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended management to:

[a] require the LDRRMO to submit annually a comprehensive LDRRMIP compliant with the requirements of RA No. 10121 and COA Circular No. 2012-002 containing the complete functional classification, list of projects and activities with sufficient details of funding whether current, continuing or special trust funds from the unexpended DRRMF of prior year;

[b] require the concerned LGU officials that in future transactions, a MOA shall be executed by and between the Municipality and the recipient barangays stating among others the purpose of the fund and their responsibilities prior to release of the fund; and

[c] require the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) reclassifying donation account to receivable account (Due from LGUs) and it should be subjected to monitoring to ensure their compliance as well as to recognize the accountability and responsibility of the concerned barangay over the project.  The receivable account shall then be eliminated upon submission of the two (2) barangays proof of their compliance, duly verified and inspected by the concerned LGU officials.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-16 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 19 | The management took into consideration the said recommendations on the preparation of LDRRMFIP and the grants made to the two (2) Barangays were monitored to ensure their compliance and a fund utilization report were submitted as a proof of their compliance.

observations.recommendation: We commended management for satisfactorily accomplishing the Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) of the Municipality and recommended to undertake best practices in adhering and implementing the provisions of the Ecological Solid Waste Management Act of 2000 and its Implementing Rules and Regulations.
observations.managementAction: AOM No. 2019-17 (2018), AAR 2018 Finding No. 20 | The MENRO-Designate in charge in accomplishing the Solid Waste Management Plan of the municipality has already submitted and complied with the requirements of NSWMC. Accordingly, the 10-year SWMP was already approved in December 2019 thru National Solid Waste Management Commission (NSWMC) Resolution No. 1059-A series of 2019 approving the 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality of Luna, Isabela

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the persons liable to settle the audit disallowances.
observations.managementAction:
AAR 2018 Finding No. 21 | A total of ₽5,000.00 was refunded during CY 2019.

A total of ₽406,193.90 representing disallowed PDAF, remained unbooked pending appeal with COA Region 02.

observations.recommendation: We commended the Municipal Focal Point System for addressing GAD issues and concerns of the Municipality. But, still, we recommended that the Focal Point System establish the Municipal GAD data base in order to identify more gender issues and concerns that need to be addressed.
observations.managementAction: According to the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), an update on the Community-Based Monitoring System (CBMS) will be conducted in CY 2019. This will be one of the bases in establishing the GAD database.
observations.reason: GAD database was not yet established as of December 31, 2018.

We recommended that Management:

demand the concerned beneficiaries to return the equipment and materials intended for their livelihood projects; and
monitor regularly the implementation of the projects to ensure compliance with the terms and conditions of the Agreement.

observations.reason: Per review of posted Performance Bond thru sampling, the reiteration is not implemented to date.

observations.recommendation: 7.1. We recommended and management positively agreed that aside from the initial water tests, they will see to it that periodic examination of water will be made.  Further, to ensure protection against contamination of water they will ask the Sangguniang Bayan through the initiative of the Municipal Health Officer to pass an ordinance on the Protection of Water Sources against contamination.
observations.managementAction: The MPDC will initiate on the conduct of regular water test/examination and will check with MBO on funding source. Further coordination with SB office and MHO on possible creation of ordinance for the protection of water sources against contamination will be done.
observations.reason: The action plan of management is for implementation.

observations.recommendation: 7.2. We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation for the WATSAN Council to perform its functions, duties and responsibilities in order to meet the goals of the SALINTUBIG project and maximize the benefits of the funds provided by the National Government.
observations.managementAction: MPDC will check on applicable Executive Order if updated or if it needs to be re-organized. MPDC will also meet with WATSAN member council for the creation of guidelines on SALINTUBIG operations.Also, MLGOO suggestion will be noted to train LGU staff for the SALINTUBIG management, instead of tapping Associations to do the same.
observations.reason: The action plan of management is for implementation.

observations.recommendation: 10.1. We recommended and Management agreed to reorganize the WATSAN to be chaired by the Municipal Mayor.  Establish and develop capacity of Water and Sanitation (WATSAN) Councils and water user associations through coordination with DILG RO No. 1.  Further, the LGU to pass an ordinance through the recommendation made by the created WATSAN for the maintenance, operation and supervision and monitoring of water supply system to ensure sustainability of water supply and services.

None

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator and the Municipal Accountant, identify the road network where the completed project belongs and make the necessary adjustments in the book of accounts of the Municipality.

We recommended that Management conduct an inventory of local roads under its jurisdiction and properly record in the books of accounts of the LGU.

We also recommended that Management enjoin the following department heads for the compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2015-008:
Municipal Accountant
Prepare the Journal Voucher (JV) to record the beginning balance of the local road network and its components in the general ledger and the Local Road Network Ledger Card (Annex D of the Circular), respectively;
Support the JV with the Registry of Public Infrastructures for Roads, working paper on the distribution of costs for the road components, and working paper on the determination of the depreciated replacement cost for road components with no available cost per registry;
Keep and maintain subsidiary records for roads and road components for every road network; and
Prepare a lapsing schedule for the computation of the depreciation for each component at the end of the year.

Municipal General Services Officer
Maintain a Local Road Inventory and Road Map; and
Keep a complete Local Road Network Property Card for all roads and its components. (Annex E of the Circular)


Municipal Engineer
Provide the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer with the complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects.

We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Accountant, the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer exert diligent efforts to locate the supporting documents necessary to establish the existence and validity of the receivable accounts and to exhaust all means to cause their collection, otherwise a request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts from the Commission on Audit will be initiated.

We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer, strictly implement the withholding of ten percent (10%) retention money on all progress billings of 50% and below or projects not satisfactorily done or not on schedule.

We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Assessor hasten the cleansing of real property taxpayer data records to enable Management to identify appropriate action in the collection of these delinquent RPTs.

We recommended that Management, thru the GAD Focal Point System adhere with the pertinent provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 specifically on the identification of GAD PPAs to ensure that they are addressing specific gender issues and concerns.
We recommended that management require the concerned officials and employees to settle promptly their suspensions and disallowances.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal Accountant recognize in the books the unrecorded disbursements totaling ₱7,349,418.79 and submit the disbursement vouchers pertaining thereto.  Likewise, the Municipal Accountant to trace the details of unreconciled difference of ₱452,932.05. Moreover, for them to strictly comply with Section 44 of NGAS for LGUs, Check Disbursement Process. In addition, the Municipal Budget Officer to update his records more often for all obligated transactions. Further, for them to reconcile their respective records at least every quarter to ensure that all transactions made will be duly accounted for. Any error, omission or misstatement appearing in the books of accounts shall be the joint responsibility of the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant.

We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately notify the payees of the issued check as declared stale. Further, the Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjustments in his books to reflect the correct balances of the affected accounts. Lastly, the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere in the provisions of Section 59 of the New Government Accounting Manual for Local Government Units Volume I in the cancellation, replacement and reporting of the stale checks.

We have recommended that management:
Establish Petty Cash Fund through a Sanggunniang Bayan Resolution withdrawn from the general fund for emergency and petty operating expenses consisting of small payments for maintenance and operating expenses which cannot be paid conveniently by check.  Replenish the same with the actual amount of expenditures when it already reached at least 75% of the amount established by submitting a replenishment voucher with all supporting documents duly summarized in a report of disbursements;
Make direct payment through check to the suppliers for transactions exceeding P15,000.00; and
Refrain from paying regular expenses like financial assistance from the petty cash fund.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive cause or order the Municipal Accountant to:
Further look into previous years’ files in order to account for the said receivables;
Provide for allowance for impairment for dormant receivables in order to have a fair presentation of the financial statements as required under PPSAS 29. Compute/determine impairment in accordance with PPSAS; and
If after all the possible means were exerted and the claims could not be accounted, request for write-off of the above receivables supported by the above-cited documents and schedules.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the periodic physical inventory of supplies or property and to submit the Report on the Physical Count of Inventories to the Auditor. Likewise, for the GSO-Designate and Accounting Office to maintain the stock cards and supplies ledger cards for each inventory and reconcile the inventories with the recorded balances in the books. Lastly, for the Municipal Accountant to refrain from recognizing the inventories as direct expense.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Budget Officer to determine the correct balances of the Current Appropriations, Continuing Appropriations and Trust Liabilities and the Municipal Accountant to adjust the amounts accordingly in the Financial Statements and Report on the Receipts and Utilization for DRRM in compliance with Section 5.1.11 COA Circular 2012-002. Further, the Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant to reconcile their records to ensure the proper inclusion and presentation of funds.

We have recommended the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately transfer the amount ₱ 2,029,703.26 from the General Fund to Special Trust Fund bank account in compliance with Section 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 to ensure actual availability of funds for Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management.

We have recommended and the management assured the audit team, thru its MDRRMC, to study and evaluate its LDRRMFIP to ensure that all programs, projects, and activities of all the different thematic areas of DRRM be properly executed as budgeted in the succeeding years to attain its intended objectives.  We further recommend the LDRRMO to provide specific schedules in the implementation of projects to serve as a guide in the preparation and execution of the LDRRMFIP.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation for the Management to monitor full implementation of the PPAs based on specific target dates contained in the PPA in order to achieve socio-economic and environmental developments and benefits intended to the constituents.

We have recommended that management refrain from granting cash advances other than for official travel to an official or employee who is not bonded to avoid possible loss and/or misappropriation of government funds.

We have recommended that management secure authorization from DILG and prepare their CapDev Agenda prior to their lakbay-aral in order to effectively and efficiently address or respond to the CapDev needs of the officials and employees, and to avoid possible wastage of government of funds.

We have recommended and the management agreed to strictly adhere that all payments be made thru check as stated in COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 and avoid paying thru cash to avoid possible loss or misappropriation of fund. Further, for the management to adopt public bidding as the general mode of procurement to promote transparency in all transactions of the municipality and to guarantee that goods/services were procured from qualified suppliers/service providers at a reasonable price.

We have recommended and the management guaranteed to:
Stop the practice of giving direct financial assistance/allowance in the implementation of SEF activities. In the allocation of SEF Fund to every school, the request for funding must be supported by specific PPAs which shall be included in the SEF budget to be implemented and procured by the Municipality itself.
Require the principals to liquidate the financial assistance supposedly for sports development amounting to ₱ 780,000.00. Procurement in relation thereto must be in compliance of the procurement law otherwise the principals shall refund the amount.
We have recommended that the Municipal Treasurer deposit intact collections daily or the next banking day to avoid possible risk of loss and/or misappropriation of funds.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the variance noted and provide the amount to be remitted to GSIS and PHIC. For the Municipal Treasurer to pay immediately the total unremitted contributions and thereafter, monitor the remittance of monthly contributions withheld from the salaries and wages of officials and employees so as to avoid penalties and charges.

Considering the foregoing, we have recommended that the Head of the Agency:
Collect the undercharged amount from suppliers/contractors that participated in the respective bidding process.
Direct the Bids and Awards committee to strictly comply with the standards set forth in RA 9184 and GPPB guidelines to avoid under collection on the proceeds from the sale of bidding documents.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Local Chief Executive instruct the OIC-Municipal Treasurer and BAC Secretariat to submit copies of Contracts and POs and supporting documents and delivery receipts and Acceptance and Inspection Reports (AIR) to the Auditor within the prescribed period.

We have recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately apply for the renewal of the fidelity bond of all the collecting officers as required under the above-cited regulations to ensure protection of government funds in case of loss/misuse.

We have recommended and the management directed the GAD Focal person to submit the DILG endorsed Annual GAD Plan and Budget and furnish the corresponding Accomplishment report within the prescribed period under COA Cir. 2014-01 to facilitate timely review and evaluation of the same.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the concerned municipal officials and employees fully implement programs/projects/activities funded under the GAD Plan and Budget to fully address gender issues/problems of the constituents. Further, for the GFPS to ensure and monitor the effective implementation of the annual GAD PPAs for the constituents to fully utilize the benefit that could be derived therefrom.

Prepare cash analysis including proof of cash schedule in order to reconcile cash against actual payables and to improve the cash position of the municipality.

We recommended that management should communicate with the concerned payees for their information as to the existence of unissued checks and draw an Adjusting Journal Entry recognizing payable account for the equivalent cost of these checks.

We recommended strict compliance with the fidelity bond requirement by applying for additional bond for Ms. Lintao.  Ensure that the amount of cash advances granted is within the limit of her maximum cash accountability.

We recommended that the designated Special Disbursing Officer be required to strictly follow the guidelines in the utilization of cash advances and stop the practice of granting cash vales as well as accommodating checks pursuant to the afore-cited provision of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure proper grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances by instructing the Municipal Accountant to:
[a] strictly monitor the cash advances of officials/employees before granting another one;
[b] make sure that said officials/employees settle their accounts within the time set by pertinent rules and regulations; and
[c] exert all efforts to enforce settlement of overdue accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasury and Municipal Accounting Offices be required to:
[a] trace the nature of the accounts Due from NGAs and Due from LGUs and send demand letters, if necessary or effect adjustment to its appropriate account classification;
[b] for Other Receivable and Loans Receivable accounts, exert effort to locate the current addresses of the borrowers and send demand letters; and
[c] if the probability of collection of these accounts is remote, file request for authority to write-off dormant receivable accounts to COA Office, duly supported with documents mentioned under paragraph 8.3 of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended the following:
[a] Direct the Inventory Committee to conduct an inventory of all Public Infrastructure assets under the LGU’s jurisdiction.  For Road Network account, use the format prescribed under COA Circular No. No. 2015-008.  Ensure that all the fields in the form be properly filled up and furnish the Audit Team a copy as basis in validating the correctness of such accounts;
[b] Direct the Municipal Accountant to fully identify these projects, determine proper classification of these accounts based on the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Sector prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009, and then make the necessary adjusting entry to transfer the CIP-Infrastructure Assets account and provide for depreciation.
[c] Determine all Public Infrastructure assets transferred to RPI which are not yet fully depreciated or impaired to be restored and recorded in the books as part of PPE accounts; and
[d] Ensure compliance with accounting and reporting guidelines set forth in COA Circular No. 2015-008 by directing the key officials to perform the duties and responsibilities assigned to them.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review/verify the Due to NGAs and Other Payable accounts and to draw the necessary adjustments, if any, and to maintain subsidiary ledger detailing all the information under these accounts.  If no record can be found, request authority from authorized officials for the reversion of these accounts to General Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant be required to:
[a] Prepare a reconciliation report of reciprocal accounts and take up the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts to ensure that all reconciling items are resolved and/or adjusted on a timely basis; and
[b] Perform constant review and appraisal of transactions to deter error and omissions.

We recommended that the Municipal Accounting office review the existing loans payable account and determine the veracity of the booked-up loan from the Department of Agriculture then make representation with the latter for the return of the equipment as embodied in the loan agreement.

We recommended that management revisit the work and financial plan and determine reason/s why until to date, the building remained unutilized.  We further recommended that power connections be checked and determine why it incurred an average monthly electrical bill of more than ₱2,000.00 despite its non-utilization.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to include these above-mentioned properties in the LGU books of accounts by drawing the necessary adjusting journal entries taking up these PPE account to its appropriate account classification.
We recommended management to cause the immediate return of unutilized balance to the Source Agency or to the Bureau of Treasury (BTr) as required in paragraph 4.9 of COA Circular No. 94-013.

[a] require the concerned barangays who were recipients of the fund transfer/financial assistance totaling ₱1,800,000.00 to submit fund utilization report duly certified by the concerned barangay officials together with the inspection report and certificate of project completion; and
[b] adjust the Donations account or the Government Equity account and take up the Due from LGUs account to recognize the accountability and responsibility of the concerned barangay over the project and furnish the Audit Team a copy of the journal entry voucher.
